steps.title: Trace the historian split
steps.content: The text begins with a broad point of agreement: historians see the Piast dynasty as crucial to forming the Polish state. It then narrows to a distinction some historians make within that dynasty. Witold Boleslawowic is presented as a documented ruler, while Radomir is presented as a disputed figure whose existence depends on a much later chronicle that relied on oral tradition. The main purpose is to explain why historians separate these cases.

steps.title: Keep the purpose focused
steps.content: Choice A invents a favorable comparison between Radomir and Witold, but the text compares documentation, not the quality of their reigns. Choice B shifts the focus to authenticating the Gesta principum Polonorum, though the chronicle is mentioned only as a source for Radomir. Choice C says the text supports a claim about Witold, who is only an example of a better-documented ruler. The remaining choice matches the whole passage: historians treat this part of Polish history differently because some figures are documented while others rely on oral tradition. The answer is D.
